children.name: Crocodylus
children.description: True crocodiles. The most sophisticated living reptiles and the closest relatives of birds. Despite their ancient lineage — over 250 million years — they show complex parental care, social communication, and even tool use. Fourth tooth of lower jaw is visible when mouth is closed — distinguishing them from alligators.

children.name: Crocodylus niloticus
children.description: The Nile crocodile is a large crocodilian native to freshwater habitats in Africa, where it is present in 26 countries. It is widely distributed in sub-Saharan Africa, occurring mostly in the eastern, southern, and central regions of the continent, and lives in different types of aquatic environments such as lakes, rivers, swamps and marshlands. It occasionally inhabits deltas, brackish lakes and rarely also saltwater.

children.name: Crocodylus porosus
children.description: The saltwater crocodile is a crocodilian native to saltwater habitats, brackish wetlands and freshwater rivers from India's east coast across Southeast Asia and the Sundaland to northern Australia and Micronesia. It has been listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List since 1996. It was hunted for its skin throughout its range up to the 1970s, and is threatened by illegal killing and habitat loss.

children.name: Crocodylus acutus
children.description: The American crocodile is a species of crocodilian found in the Neotropics. It is the most widespread of the four extant species of crocodiles from the Americas, with populations present from South Florida, the Caribbean islands of Cuba, Jamaica, and Hispaniola, and the coasts of Mexico to as far south as Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Venezuela.

children.name: Crocodylus moreletii
children.description: Freshwater species of Central America.

children.name: Crocodylus palustris
children.description: A broad-snouted freshwater crocodile of the Indian subcontinent, frequenting rivers, lakes, and man-made reservoirs; known to dig burrows to escape heat and drought.

children.name: Crocodylus johnstoni
children.description: A slim-snouted Australian crocodile of inland rivers and billabongs; relatively harmless to humans and capable of a bounding gallop on land.

children.name: Crocodylus siamensis
children.description: A critically endangered freshwater crocodile of Southeast Asia, nearly extinct in the wild but widely bred on farms and the basis of hybrid stock.

children.name: Crocodylus rhombifer
children.description: A small, agile and notably aggressive crocodile restricted to Cuba's Zapata and Lanier swamps, capable of leaping from the water and exhibiting unusual pack-like hunting.

children.name: Crocodylus intermedius
children.description: One of the largest predators in the Americas, confined to the Orinoco basin of Colombia and Venezuela and critically endangered after decades of hide hunting.

children.name: Crocodylus mindorensis
children.description: A small freshwater crocodile endemic to the Philippines and among the most endangered crocodilians on Earth, with only a few hundred adults surviving in the wild.

children.name: Crocodylus novaeguineae
children.description: A freshwater crocodile of northern New Guinea, largely nocturnal and central to traditional crocodile-skin economies and ceremonial scarification cultures.

children.name: Crocodylus suchus
children.description: Long lumped with the Nile crocodile but genetically distinct and more docile; the sacred crocodile mummified by the ancient Egyptians at Kom Ombo.

children.name: Crocodylus halli
children.description: Described in 2019 from southern New Guinea after being recognised as distinct from the New Guinea crocodile of the north, separated by the island's central highlands.

children.name: Mecistops
children.description: Slender-snouted crocodiles of West and Central Africa, with extremely narrow, fish-catching jaws.

children.name: Mecistops cataphractus
children.description: A reclusive forest-river crocodile with a long thin snout adapted for catching fish, now critically endangered and recently split from its Central African relative.

children.name: Osteolaemus
children.description: Dwarf crocodiles.

children.name: Osteolaemus tetraspis
children.description: World's smallest crocodile at 1.5 m; heavily armoured; nocturnal forest dweller.

children.name: Gavialis
children.description: The gharial.

children.name: Gavialis gangeticus
children.description: Extremely long narrow snout with 110 interlocking teeth for catching fish; male develops bulbous ghara on snout tip used for vocalisation; IUCN Critically Endangered with fewer than 1,000 wild; sometimes placed in its own family Gavialidae.
